'''Paul Cooke''' (born 17 April 1981) is an English former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1990s, 2000s and 2010s, and rugby league, and [[rugby union]] [[Coach (sports)|coach]] of the 2010s.

He is currently Head coach at [[Featherstone Rovers]].

He played at representative level for [[England national rugby league team|England]], and at club level in the [[Super League]] for [[Hull FC]], [[Hull Kingston Rovers]], and [[Wakefield Trinity]], and in the [[League 1 (rugby league)|Championship 1]] and [[Championship (rugby league)|Championship]] for [[Doncaster R.L.F.C.|Doncaster]] and [[Featherstone Rovers]], as a {{rlp|so}}, or {{rlp|lf}}, and has coached rugby league (RL) for [[Doncaster R.L.F.C.|Doncaster]], and the [[Leigh Centurions]] (assistant coach), and rugby union (RU) for the [[Doncaster Knights]] (Skills, and Backs Coach).

==Background==
Cooke was born in East [[Kingston upon Hull|Hull]], [[Humberside]].

==Playing career==
===Hull FC===
Cooke made his first team debut for [[Hull F.C.|Hull]] against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] in April 1999.<ref>{{cite web |title=Rugby League Icons: Paul Cooke |url=https://www.hullfc.com/blog/2020/04/25/rugby-league-icons-paul-cooke/ |website=Hull F.C. |access-date=22 March 2025}}</ref>

His pairing with [[Richard Horne]] led to the memorable victory over [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] in the [[2005 Challenge Cup]] Final in which Cooke scored the winning [[Try (rugby)|try]] to give Hull FC a 25–24 victory.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/4169230.stm|publisher=BBC News|title=Hull stun Leeds in Challenge Cup|date=27 August 2005}}</ref> Hull FC reached the [[2006 Super League Grand final]] to be contested against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]], and Cooke played {{rlp|so}} in his side's 4–26 loss.<ref>{{cite news|title=St Helens 26–4 Hull FC|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/6035553.stm|access-date=18 August 2013|publisher=[[BBC News]]|date=14 October 2006}}</ref>

On 22 July after being given a franchise for [[Super League]] from 2009 to 2011 [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] coach [[Shaun McRae]] said he would be interested in speaking to Cooke with an intention of him joining his side, Cooke and McRae worked together at Hull FC earlier in their careers, though Cooke did not go on to play for the Salford City Reds.

===Hull Kingston Rovers===
On 19 April 2007, [[BBC Look North (East Yorkshire and Lincolnshire)]] announced that Cooke was to walk out on [[Hull FC]] to join cross city rivals [[Hull Kingston Rovers]]. Cooke was born in East Hull where Hull KR are based and supported the club as a youngster. He played his last game for Hull F.C. against [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford]] that night. In his autobiography many years later, Cooke revealed that he was unhappy with the club for failing to honour an agreement that they would apply for his [[testimonial game|testimonial]] in 2008.<ref>{{cite web |title=Bygones: Paul Cooke breaks silence over switch to Hull KR and Hull FC's 2006 Grand Final dispute |url=https://www.yorkshirepost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/bygones-paul-cooke-breaks-silence-over-switch-to-hull-kr-and-hull-fcs-2006-grand-final-dispute-1789784 |website=Yorkshire Post |access-date=22 March 2025 |date=10 October 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Cooke |first1=Paul |last2=Durham |first2=Adrian |title=Judas: The Story of Paul Cooke |date=2016 |publisher=Pitch Publishing |isbn=978-1-78531-205-2 |pages=115–135 |url=https://archive.org/details/judas0000cook/page/114/mode/2up?view=theater}}</ref>

On 23 April 2007, it was announced that Cooke had signed a three-and-a-half-year deal with Hull Kingston Rovers, taking effect immediately. The [[Rugby Football League]] (RFL) initially blocked this transfer<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/hull/6584157.stm Cooke secures switch to Hull KR] BBC News – 27 April 2007</ref> stating that his registration remained with Hull FC. Some confusion followed amid allegations that he had never signed his contract with Hull FC, despite local media reports at the time to the contrary. However the move went ahead and he duly turned up playing for [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] the following week.

In November 2007, a RFL tribunal found Cooke guilty of misconduct for approaching [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] while still under contract to [[Hull FC]]. His punishment included a ban that ruled him out of the first six games of the 2008 season.<ref>{{cite web |title=Illegal approach lands Cooke a four-month ban |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2007/nov/15/rugbyleague.sport |website=The Guardian |access-date=22 March 2025 |date=15 November 2007}}</ref>

Following the tribunal decision Hull F.C. chief executive James Rule said "We will now take time to consider our next steps with regard to potential future civil action" suggesting the Cooke transfer saga may be far from over. However, it died down, and by the end of the 2009 season Cooke had settled into the Hull Kingston Rovers team, making a major contribution to his team's next two Super League finishes of 7th and 4th.

===Wakefield Trinity===
[[Wakefield Trinity Wildcats|Wakefield Trinity]] signed Cooke from Hull Kingston Rovers until the end of the 2010–11 season, the 28-year-old {{rlp|so}}/{{rlp|lf}}, had been out of favour at Hull Kingston Rovers that season. Cooke made his début with [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield]] against [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] on Sunday 21 March 2010, and made his full début in the 36–16 defeat at Warrington, and his first full home game when Wakefield Trinity won 19–6 against neighbours [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]].

Cooke had the option to earn a deal for next season, whether that was at [[Wakefield Trinity]] or another club.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_6037944,00.html|work=Sky Sports|title=Cooke seals Wildcats switch|date=19 March 2010}}</ref>

===Doncaster RLFC===
After spending a year away from rugby league for personal reasons,<ref>{{cite web |title=Doncaster chase Paul Cooke |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/doncaster-chase-paul-cooke |website=Love Rugby League |access-date=22 March 2025 |date=15 December 2011}}</ref> Cooke signed for Championship 1 side [[Doncaster RLFC]] in January 2012, after fans and sponsors agreed to pay half his salary. He combined playing for the club with a full-time job in their development department.<ref>{{cite web |title=Fans help Doncaster sign cup winner Paul Cooke |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/16535359 |website=BBC Sport |access-date=22 March 2025 |date=12 January 2012}}</ref> He also signed as a backs coach for [[Doncaster Knights]], the town's rugby union club, for the 2013/14 season.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.drfc.co.uk/About-Us/News/2013/June/Cooke-to-join-Coaching-Staff-4321 |title=Cooke to join Coaching Staff |accessdate=5 September 2013 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20130629094454/http://www.drfc.co.uk/About-Us/News/2013/June/Cooke-to-join-Coaching-Staff-4321 |archivedate=29 June 2013  }}</ref>

===Featherstone Rovers===
Cooke made his début for [[Featherstone Rovers]] on Sunday 28 June 2015, and he played his last match for Featherstone during the 2015 season.

==Coaching career==
===Featherstone Rovers===
On 27 February 2025 he was announced as the new head coach, following the sacking of [[James Ford (rugby league)|James Ford]] the day before, with [[Ged Corcoran]] as assistant<ref>{{cite web|website=Total RL|date=27 February 2025|first=Callum |last=Walker|title=Featherstone Rovers announce new head coach and assistant following James Ford’s sacking|url=https://www.totalrl.com/featherstone-rovers-announce-new-head-coach-and-assistant-following-james-fords-sacking/}}</ref>
